coredata.dc:title: Stimulus response of HNT-CDs-Eu nano-sensor: Toward visual point-of-care monitoring of a bacterial spore biomarker with hypersensitive multi-color agarose gel based analytical device
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Xiangzhen
Jia, Lei
Zhang, Lina
Li, Yongxin
Xu, Jun
coredata.dc:description: Ultrasensitive visual detection of anthrax markers is of great significance for disease prevention and early warning of terrorist attacks. A novel of dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent nano-probe (HNT-CDs-Eu) has been elaborately tailored based on the direct in-situ growth of CDs on natural mineral nanotubes (HNTs) for the determination and visual assay of DPA concentrations, using DTPA as the efficient chelating agent and the raw material for the preparation of CDs. The rich carboxyl groups of DTPA also provide coordination sites for Eu3+ to form highly sensitive recognition groups. This multi-color nano-sensor can not only realize ultra-sensitive trace detection of pure DPA, but also meet the feasibility of visual sensing of DPA in environmental water samples. Since the common filter paper has strong blue fluorescence under ultraviolet light irradiation at 365 nm, a color agarose gel for visual inspection and an advanced analytical device for one-to-two decoder are further prepared to realize the intelligent and real-time detection of DPA.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Multi-color fluorescence
Bacterial spore biomarker
Halloysite nanotubes
Point-of-care monitoring
Intelligent sensing

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The in-situ growth of CDs onto the surface of HNT
Preparation of rare earth functionalized HNT-CDs-Eu
Visualized detection of DPA by gel-modified nano-sensor
Measurement of DPA in actual environmental water
The structure and morphology characterization of HNT-CDs-Eu nano-sensor
Polychromatic fluorescence sensing mechanism of DPA
Optimization of optimal mensuration conditions for DPA
Identification of DPA in actual samples
Construction of agarose gel device and visual recognition of DPA
Application of fluorescence sensing system in logic decoder

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM diagrams of (a) HNT and (b) HNT-CDs-Eu. (c) EDX spectrum of HNT-CDs-Eu. (d) FTIR spectra of HNT, HNT-CDs and HNT-CDs-Eu.
XRD spectra of HNT, HNT-CDs and HNT-CDs-Eu (a), high resolution XPS spectra of HNT-CDs-Eu: full spectrum (b), C 1s (c) and O 1s (d).

(a) The fluorescence spectra and (b) CIE chromaticity coordinates of HNT-CDs-Eu and HNT-CDs-Eu-DPA. (c) Energy transfer mechanism diagram and (d) time-resolved fluorescence spectra. The illustration in (a) were the corresponding pictures under 254 nm UV lamp.

(a) Fluorescence spectra, (b) fluorescence magnification of low concentration DPA (0–5 μM). (c) the piecewise fitting linear relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio (I616/I445) and DPA concentration of (0–7 μM and 7–20 μM). (d) CIE chromaticity diagram in the presence of different concentrations of DPA. (e) The fluorescence image under UV lamp of 254 nm (the concentration of DPA was 0, 0.5, 1, 3, 5, 7, 10, 15, 20 μM) and (f) the effect of interference on I616/I445 (the concentration of DPA and interferer were fixed at 5 μM).

The fluorescence color images of HNT-CDs-Eu agarose gel under different concentrations of DPA and 254 nm ultraviolet lamp (upon), the function diagram between red (R) and blue (B) ratio to DPA concentration obtained by chromaticity analysis (down).

(a) Simple schematic diagram of logical decoder, (b) truth table of input and output signals, (c) histogram of normalized fluorescence intensity emitted at I616 and I445, and (d) truth table of logical decoder.
Schematic diagram of construction and DPA detection of HNT-CDs-Eu nano-sensor.
Comparison of the limit of detection of fluorescent sensors for the detection of DPA as a biomarker of B. anthracis.
The ratio fluorescence probe is used to analyze the results of DPA in actual environmental water samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Stimulus response of HNT-CDs-Eu nano-sensor: Toward visual point-of-care monitoring of a bacterial spore biomarker with hypersensitive multi-color agarose gel based analytical device

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, No. 2001 Shiji Road, Jiaozuo, Henan 454000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, No. 2001 Shiji Road Jiaozuo, Henan, 454000, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Henan Polytechnic University, #2001 Shiji Street, Jiaozuo, Henan 454000, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ultrasensitive visual detection of anthrax markers is of great significance for disease prevention and early warning of terrorist attacks. A novel of dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent nano-probe (HNT-CDs-Eu) has been elaborately tailored based on the direct in-situ growth of CDs on natural mineral nanotubes (HNTs) for the determination and visual assay of DPA concentrations, using DTPA as the efficient chelating agent and the raw material for the preparation of CDs. The rich carboxyl groups of DTPA also provide coordination sites for Eu3+ to form highly sensitive recognition groups. This multi-color nano-sensor can not only realize ultra-sensitive trace detection of pure DPA, but also meet the feasibility of visual sensing of DPA in environmental water samples. Since the common filter paper has strong blue fluorescence under ultraviolet light irradiation at 365 nm, a color agarose gel for visual inspection and an advanced analytical device for one-to-two decoder are further prepared to realize the intelligent and real-time detection of DPA.

ce:section.ce:para: Anthrax is an acute zoonotic infection caused by the gram-positive Bacillus anthracis, which is infected by dormant spores. Bacillus anthracis spores have a high degree of environmental adaptability. Even after experiencing extreme environments such as high temperature, radiation, strong acid, and strong alkali, they can germinate as long as the surrounding environment is suitable [1,2]. After germination, spores are highly infectious, and humans can be infected by direct or indirect contact with spores, with a fatality rate of more than 90%, which makes them a potential biological warfare agent [3–5]. Therefore, the rapid detection of anthrax spores is of great significance for disease prevention and control and early warning of terrorist attacks. As a major component of Bacillus anthracis spores [6,7], 2,6-pyridine dicarboxylic acid (DPA) is a bacterial marker. Therefore, it is particularly important to build some sensing platforms or develop detection tools to achieve rapid, sensitive, highly selective and visual DPA detection.

Numerous scholars have been doing a lot of research on visual DPA detection for a long time. Although some biological detection and chemiluminescence detection strategies have achieved satisfactory results, they still have defects such as long detection cycle, complex processing, high cost and tedious analysis [8,9]. Recently developed detection methods based on multi-color fluorescence sensing have become one of the most promising methods for quantitative evaluation of analytes due to their advantages such as high sensitivity, good selectivity, rapid detection, convenient portability and low cost [10–12]. These kind of ratio fluorescence sensors have built-in self-calibration mechanism, which can eliminate the influence of background interference and irrelevant signals, avoid false negative or false positive results, and improve the sensitivity and selectivity of detection results.

The design of the ratio fluorescence sensor usually selects two different emission bands of the fluorophore as the detection signal, but the fluorescence probe based on traditional organic dye usually has only one emission wavelength, which gradually cannot satisfied the requirement of high sensitivity detection. Emerging fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) are attracting increasing attention due to their low toxicity, good biocompatibility and photostability [13–17]. Importantly, the surface of CDs often contains a large number of easily modified functional groups (-COOH, -OH, -NH2), resulting the great application potential [18,19]. At present, CDs-based ratio fluorescence sensors mostly adopt the method of modifying the synthetic CDs on the carrier or directly modification on CDs, while the strategy of in situ growing CDs on the substrate is rare. This new strategy can avoid the complex problems caused by the small particle size of CDs, low quantum yield, and unstable excitation and emission wavelengths in the process of subsequent application. Meanwhile, rare earth ions have attracted much attention in the construction of luminescent materials due to their unique photophysical properties, such as 4 f sub-electron layer configuration, large Stokes shift and long fluorescence lifetime [20–23]. Europium and terbium ions, in particular, can be sensitized to luminescence by the effective energy transferred by DPA through the “antenna effect”. Compared with Tb3+, it is found that the fluorescence emission of Eu3+ is located in the red region and can eliminate its second-order scattering interference at the place of maximum fluorescence emission [24]. Therefore, it is particularly important to select a suitable carrier to integrate CDs with rare earth complexes.

Halloysite nanotube (HNT) is a kind of natural clay with hollow nanostructure and rich reserves in nature. There are a large number of hydroxyl groups on the outer surface of HNT, which are negatively charged, while the alumina group in the inner surface makes it positively charged [25–28]. This unique property makes HNT easy to functionalize and form various organic-inorganic hybrid materials. For example, Nirmal et al. successfully selectively fabricated AuNC@HNT-In and AuNC@HNT-Out on the inner and outer surfaces of HNTs for the catalytic reduction of nitrophenol [29]. Xu et al. [30] prepared Ag nanoparticles through in-situ synthesis method inside the HNT, and a layer of rare earth complex with potential recognition function for tetracycline was modified on its outer surface to realize the synchronous detection and degradation of antibiotics. More importantly, compared with other nano-synthetic carriers, HNT has high thermal stability, good ecological compatibility and non-toxic characteristics, and is a high-quality carrier for the construction of inorganic hybrid materials.

Inspired by the characteristics of the above nanomaterials, a layer of fluorescent CDs has been prepared on the surface of HNT by in-situ synthesis in this study. Meanwhile, a layer of rare earth nanomaterials with potential sensing properties for DPA is also further loaded on the outer surface of HNT-CDs by covalent grafting modification, which is used for rapid, sensitive and high selective determination of DPA. As shown in Scheme 1, diethylenetriamine pentaacetic acid (DTPA) is not only an efficient chelating agent, but also rich in nitrogen sources and carboxyl groups. It can synthesize carbon dots by itself and provide active coordination points for rare earth europium ions. One method for rapid detection of DPA is established after sepharose gel device model assembly. The method report here has high reliability and reproducibility in the application of DPA content determination in actual environmental water samples. Furthermore, a new semi-quantitative analysis strategy for DPA content in samples was established with the help of smartphone chroma analysis App to evaluate the rapid visual detection performance of DPA.

The reagents including halloysite nanotube (HNT), 3-chloropropyltriethoxysilane (CPTEOS), xylene, Polyethyleneimine (PEI), diethylenetriamine pentaacetic acid (DTPA), 2,6-pyrrolidine dicarboxylic acid (DPA), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimidehy-drochloride (EDC), europium nitrate hexahydrate (Eu(NO3)3٠6H2O), tris(hydroxymethylmetyl)aminomethane (Tris), and hydrochloric acid (HCl) were purchased commercially and can be used directly without pretreatment. The water used in the experiment was ultrapure water.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) and energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) were characterized by Tecnai-G2-F30 under 300 kV acceleration voltage. The powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) data was measured on a Rigaku Dmax 2400 diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation in the 2θ range of 5–90° with scan rate of 10°/min. The fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) of materials (in 4000–400 cm−1 wave number range) was determined using mixing solid powder with KBr particles and pressing them into transparent sheets technique by a Brooke V70 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer. The steady-state emission spectra were obtained by F97 Pro fluorescence spectrometer at 282 nm excitation wavelength. The time-resolved fluorescence spectrum were investigated by Edinburgh FS5 equipped with a cooled R928P photomultiplier tube.

PEI was grafted on the outer surface of HNT nanotubes according to the method described in references [31,32] and some improvements were made. Firstly, 1 g of HNT powder was added to 50 mL of xylene solution, ally stirred at room temperature for 1 h, and then 5 mL of CPTEOS was added drop by drop. After refluxing in oil bath at 80 °C for 8 h, the chlorinated HNT precipitation after centrifugation was washed several times with anhydrous ethanol. Then, it was dispersed in 40 mL of deionized water, stirred at room temperature for 30 min, and then added 2 g of PEI, refluxed in oil bath at 90 °C for 8 h. The final product was collected by centrifugation and washed with ethanol and water alternately for several times before vacuum drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The in-situ growth of CDs onto the surface of HNT
ce:section.ce:para: A total amount of 786 mg DTPA, 230 mg of NHS and 383 mg of EDC were added into 30 mL of DMF and water (v/v = 1:1) mixture, and stirred for 4 h to fully activate the carboxyl group of DTPA. Then 250 mg of amino-functionalized HNT-NH2 was added and stirred for 24 h at room temperature. After centrifugation, the collected solid was re-dispersed in 30 mL of deionized water. After stirring for 30 min, the solid was uniformly dispersed and transferred to a 50 mL stainless steel liner PTFE reactor. The reaction was carried out at 200 ℃ for 10 h and the solid precipitate with blue fluorescence was obtained by centrifugation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of rare earth functionalized HNT-CDs-Eu
ce:section.ce:para: All the HNT-CDs samples obtained in the previous experiment were dispersed in 10 mL ethanol, and then 5 mL ethanol solution containing 200 mg of Eu(NO3)3·6H2O was added. After reaction in oil bath at 60 °C for 5 h, the solid was collected by centrifugation, washed several times and dried in vacuum for further use.

200 μg of HNT-CDs-Eu aqueous solution was placed in a fluorescent colorimetric dish. Tris-HCl buffer with pH = 9 was added to make the total volume of the solution reach 2 mL, and then the concentration of DPA was gradually increased. The fluorescence intensity was recorded by fluorescence spectrometer at 282 nm. Similar methods were used for the determination of DPA in actual water environment samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visualized detection of DPA by gel-modified nano-sensor
ce:section.ce:para: 250 mg of agarose was added into 10 mL of Tris-HCl buffer solution with pH= 9. After heating and dissolving at 100 °C, 150 mg of HNT-CDs-Eu nano-sensor was added and continue stirred for 10 min. The obtained mixture was uniformly dropped into the mold, and the gel containing luminescent materials was obtained after cooling. The color change of the gel-modified nano-sensor with the increase of DPA concentration was recorded under 254 nm UV lamp.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of DPA in actual environmental water
ce:section.ce:para: Local tap water, lake water and soil were taken for actual sample detection. Treatment of water samples: DPA test was performed after removing insoluble matter by 0.22 µm membrane filtration. Treatment of soil samples: 2.0 g of soil samples were dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water, ultrasonically treated for 30 min and centrifuged at high speed for 10 min. The supernatant was filtered with 0.22 µm membrane before subsequent experiments. 200 μg of HNT-CDs-Eu powder was dissolved in Tris-HCl buffer at pH = 9 and mixed with the actual sample. Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at the excitation wavelength of 282 nm, and the concentration and recovery of DPA in the actual sample were analyzed by fitting the working curve. Each sample was measured three times at room temperature and then the relative standard deviation (RSD) was calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The structure and morphology characterization of HNT-CDs-Eu nano-sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The microstructure of HNT and HNT-CDs-Eu were studied by electron microscopy. The solid-state fluorescence quenching of amorphous CDs can be inhibited after confinement [33–35], and HNT-CDs-Eu was prepared by the coordination strategy of rare earth Eu3+ with HNT in situ restricted CDs. As shown in Fig. 1a, it can be clearly observed that the initial HNT was a cylindrical tube with multi-layer smooth walls and an open tube cavity [36]. However, when the carbon dots were anchored to the surface of HNT by in-situ growth method and further coordinated with europium, many small particles with sizes of about 2–3 nm appeared on the surface of HNT. At the same time, the official cavity surface of HNT became rough, indicating that CDs and europium complexes were successfully modified to HNT (Fig. 1b). Furthermore, the chemical composition and elemental distribution of HNT-CDs-Eu were investigated by EDX-mapping and the energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), as shown in Figs. S1 and 1c, in which the distributions of C, N, O, Si and Eu elements were displayed. The EDX spectrum of the HNT-CDs-Eu exhibited the presence of Eu element, which proved that Eu coordinated with the carboxyl group on HNT-CDs successfully.

The FTIR spectra of HNT, HNT-CDs and HNT-CDs-Eu are shown in Fig. 1d. The strong absorption peaks at 3697, 3618 and 909 cm−1 were caused by Si-OH vibration of HNT [37], and the absorption peaks at 1030 cm−1 was due to the stretching vibration of Si-O [38]. By comparing the infrared spectra of HNT-CDs-Eu and HNT-CDs, it was found that the CO and C-O vibration intensity of COO- decreased at 1649 and 1385 cm−1 [39], and a new peak appeared at 1586 cm−1, which proved that the carboxyl group was successfully coordinated with Eu3+. The absorption peak at 1384 cm−1 can be attributed to the strong absorption peak [40,41] caused by the coordination of NO3- and Eu3+, which also fully proved the successful preparation of HNT-CDs-Eu nano-sensor.

In order to further study the crystal structure of the material, powder X-ray diffraction tests were carried out on HNT, HNT-CDs and HNT-CDs-Eu. As shown in Fig. 2a, the strong diffraction peaks of HNT at 12.22°, 19.85°, 24.79°, 34.98° and 38.45° were clearly shown in the spectra of the above three nanomaterials. These typical characteristic peaks affiliated to (001), (020), (002), (130) and (132) crystal planes of HNT (JCPDS# 29–1487) [42–44], respectively. The diffraction peaks in the range of 45°–65° originated from impurities in clay, which indicated that the crystal structure of HNT was not changed during the formation of carbon dots and the grafting of europium complexes. HNT-CDs and HNT-CDs-Eu had a wide peak at 20.73°, which can be attributed to the highly disordered and amorphous CDs structure on the surface of HNT [45].

The surface composition of HNT-CDs-Eu nanomaterials was further analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy of HNT-CDs-Eu. As shown in Fig. 2b, it can be obviously found that there were reflection peaks at 1136.10, 532.21, 402.40, 284.45, 103.85 and 74.22 eV, which subordinated to Eu 3d, O 1s, N 1s, C 1s, Si 2p and Al 2p, respectively. Although the weak characteristic peak of Eu 3D was due to the low capacity of Eu, it can still prove the successful preparation of HNT-CDS-Eu nano-sensors. Since the surface of HNT-CDs-Eu had DTPA-like functional groups, four characteristic peaks of C-C/C-H, C-N, C-O and CO groups at 284.5, 285.2, 286.0 and 286.8 eV, respectively, can also be seen in the high-resolution XPS diagram of C1s (Fig. 2c) [46,47]. At the same time, in the O 1s pattern (Fig. 2d), the reflection peaks of 531.7, 532.48, 533.13, 533.75 and 534.35 eV were belonged to O-N, Si-O [48], C-OH, CO and C-O groups respectively.

To confirm that CDs and Eu were formed on the outer surface of HNTs, we conducted a more in-depth explanation of the experimental process. Firstly, PEI was covalently grafted onto the surface of HNT after surface chlorination of HNT. Secondly, the covalent grafting of DTPA was realized on the surface of amino HNT by amidation, and the DTPA-modified HNT was obtained. Finally, the in-situ formation of CDs on HNT surface was promoted by hydrothermal reaction. Since the surface of HNT-CDs obtained after reaction still contained a large number of carboxyl groups, it can provide coordination points for Eu3+. In order to prove the successful implementation of the above steps, we investigated the fluorescence spectra of the key reactants and products. As shown in Fig. S2, it can be clearly seen that DTPA itself had no fluorescence and no response to DPA. The pure DTPA CDs had strong blue fluorescence near 445 nm and had no response to DPA. The emission peak of HNT-CDs-Eu near 445 nm can be attributed to the emission of DTPA CDs, and the low intensity was due to the low net content of CDs in the same amount of HNT-CDs-Eu. Although Eu3+ had a response to DPA, its response ability was significantly lower than that of HNT-CDs-Eu nano-probe. In other words, after a series of reactions such as chlorination, aminozation, amidation and hydrothermal treatment at high temperature, HNT did not show fluorescence until it showed blue fluorescence under 254 nm UV lamp, which fully proved the formation of CDs. In addition, the HNT-CDs-Eu obtained after hydrothermal treatment was washed with deionized water and ethanol for several times to remove free CDs, and the fluorescence still existed, which proved that the product was not a mixture of HNT and CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Polychromatic fluorescence sensing mechanism of DPA
ce:section.ce:para: In order to clarify the sensing mechanism of DPA, the fluorescence response mechanism of the nano-sensor to DPA at the excitation wavelength of 282 nm was studied. As shown in Fig. 3a and b, in the absence of DPA, the nano-sensor showed only blue fluorescence emission of CDs at 445 nm (0.233, 0.232). At this time, the fluorescence of Eu3+ was quenched due to the nonradiative quenching of water molecules in DTPA-Eu complex. When the sample contains DPA, Eu3+ in the nano-sensor will coordinate with DPA and trigger the ultrasensitive energy transition of Eu3+ at 616 nm by antenna effect, resulting in strong red fluorescence (0.442, 0.295). When the concentration of DPA reached 5 μM, the ratio fluorescence sensor showed a significant fluorescence color mutation from blue to red, which provided a prerequisite for the visual analysis of DPA. The blue fluorescence of CDs at 445 nm in the nano-sensor was almost unaffected by the DPA concentration, which could be used as a stable reference signal. By recording the fluorescence intensity ratio of Eu3+ at 616 nm and CDs at 445 nm with the change of DPA concentration, the sensitivity and practicability of the nano-sensor for DPA can be evaluated.

The reason why the nano-sensor can open the red-light channel can be attributed to the following two aspects. First, the lowest triplet energy level of DPA was higher than the resonant energy level of Eu3+ [49–51], and the energy level difference between DPA and Eu3+ can well meet the strong coordination of DPA and Eu3+, resulting in effective energy transfer and Eu3+ sensitized luminescence [52] (Fig. 3c). Second, DPA can replace the coordinated H2O molecules and effectively inhibit the non-radiative quenching effect. The fluorescence lifetimes of Eu3+ in HNT-CDs-Eu and HNT-CDs-Eu-DPA calculated by time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy can verify the above point. As indicated in Fig. 3d, the average fluorescence decay time of Eu3+ in HNT-CDs-Eu-DPA was 0.947 ms, which was significantly prolonged compared with 0.176 ms of HNT-CDs-Eu. This was because DPA replaced the water molecules in the first coordination sphere of rare earth ions, inhibited the nonradiative quenching effect, and prolonged the excited state lifetime of Eu3+. At the same time, the lifetime attenuation of the nan-sensor in deuterated water (
τ
D
2
O
) was also measured, and the replaced number of water molecules (q) after DPA coordination was calculated to be 5 (the corresponding photophysical data were shown in Table S1) by using the empirical formula [53,54]:
(1)
q = 1.1 [
τ
H
2
O
− 1
−
τ
D
2
O
− 1
− 0.31 ]

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of optimal mensuration conditions for DPA
ce:section.ce:para: In order to ensure the highest sensitivity and lowest detection limit of DPA, the detection conditions need to be optimized. Firstly, the photostability of the probe in different pH buffers and the time stability under high pressure xenon lamp irradiation were evaluated (Figs. S3 and S4). The results showed that the probe had good fluorescence stability. After that, the response of HNT-CDs-Eu to DPA in Tris-HCl buffer at different pH values was discussed. As shown in Fig. S5, the fluorescence emission intensity ratio (I616/I445) at 616 nm and 445 nm reached the maximum when the pH of the mixed system of nano-sensor and DPA reached 9. With the increase of pH, the fluorescence intensity ratio decreased significantly, which may be related to the formation of hydroxide precipitation by Eu3+ under strong alkaline conditions. The above results showed that the best working environment of the nano-sensor should be weak alkaline, so Tris-HCl buffer (pH=9) was selected as the optimal detection condition. In addition, the response time of the sensor was also the key to evaluate the sensing performance. Figs. S6 and S7 show that the response of the nanosensor to DPA had good stability, and the fastest response time was 30 s, which provided a prerequisite for rapid visual recognition for the practical detection of bacterial spores.

The sensitivity was an important criterion to measure the performance of the fluorescence nano-sensor. Under the optimal detection conditions, the DPA response was tested by fluorescence titration. As shown in Fig. 4a, with the increase of DPA contents, Eu3+ was continuously sensitized, and the intensity of characteristic emission peaks at 593 nm, 616 nm (with the most significant change in fluorescence intensity), 650 nm and 693 nm gradually increased, resulting in the transition of fluorescence color from blue to red. However, even at high DPA concentration (20 μM), the emission intensity of CDs at 445 nm was basically unchanged, indicating that the change of fluorescence color in the sensing process was mainly caused by the antenna effect of Eu3+. It can be seen from the low concentration amplification diagram in Fig. 4b that the minimum detection concentration was 0.01 μM, which realized the ultra-sensitive detection of DPA. The intensity ratio of I616/I445 showed a good linear relationship with the increase of DPA concentration in the range of 0–7 μM and 7–20 μM, and the correlation coefficients were 0.99664 and 0.99911, respectively (Fig. 4c). The minimum detection limit (LOD) of DPA calculated according to the three-fold signal-to-noise ratio was 5 nM, which was far lower than the infection dose of Bacillus anthracis spores to human body (60 μM) [55–57]. Compared with the published ratio fluorescence detection method ( Table 1), the probe showed higher sensitivity. More importantly, the synthesis method of the probe was novel and employed natural, environmentally friendly and low-cost precursors.

The CIE colorimetry coordinates were obtained by integral calculation of the fluorescence emission spectra of HNT-CDs-Eu nano-sensor at different DPA concentrations (Table S2). As shown in the chromaticity coordinate diagram of Fig. 4d, with the increase of DPA concentration, the fluorescence color of HNT-CDs-Eu nano-sensor gradually evolved from blue to red, showing the tunable change of fluorescence color. As shown in Fig. 4e, the varying regularity of fluorescence color captured under 254 nm UV lamp was basically consistent with that in the color coordinate diagram, indicating that the prepared nano-sensor possessed definite practical application in the naked eye recognition of DPA.

Although the prepared HNT-CDs-Eu nano-sensor had good detection effect for DPA, considering the complexity of the actual sample, it was necessary to eliminate or reduce the influence of the presence of other interfering substances on the detection results, and prove that the probe had the application potential of specific recognition of DPA in actual samples. The effects of some DPA analogs such as benzoic acid (BA), pyridinecarboxylic acid (PA), nicotinic acid (NA), terephthalic acid (p-PA), isophthalic acid (m-PA), isonicotinic acid (INA) and 1,3,5-tricarboxylic acid (HTA), amino acids such as L-aspartic acid (Asp), L-glutamic acid (Glu), glycine (Gly), cysteine (Cys) and some common ions (K+, Na+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Al3+ and Fe3+) on the fluorescence of the nano-sensor were investigated. Figs. 4f and S8. showed that the effect of these potential interfering substances on the fluorescence intensity ratio of the probe can be ignored, which indicated that the nano-sensor had the ability of specific selection for DPA in complex samples. This excellent selectivity was due to the fact that the nitrogen atom in the pyridine ring of DPA molecule and the oxygen atoms in the two carboxylic acid groups can provide three strong coordination sites with Eu3+ and replace all water molecules. In summary, the synthesized HNT-CDs-Eu nano-sensor had high sensitivity, excellent selectivity and good anti-interference capability for DPA detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification of DPA in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the feasibility of HNT-CDs-Eu ratio fluorescent nano-sensor in practical application, it was applied to the analysis of bacterial spore marker DPA in tap water, lake water and soil. To ensure the accuracy of the experimental results, each experiment was repeated at least three times, and then the average value was calculated. Table 2 indicated that the reproducibility of the nano-sensor was up to 93%–110%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was less than 3.2%. It had high accuracy and reliability for the determination of DPA in actual environmental water samples, illustrating the feasibility and reliability of this assay for the detection of bacterial spore markers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of agarose gel device and visual recognition of DPA
ce:section.ce:para: In view of the advantages of high sensitivity, good selectivity and obvious color change of fluorescence signal, a portable visual sensor based on agarose gel was developed. Since the initial fluorescence signal of the HNT-CDs-Eu nano-sensor was blue and relatively weak, the use of filter paper as a commonly used sensor carrier will lead to the overlap of the background fluorescence signal and the fluorescence signal of the nano-sensor. The discoloration of ordinary filter paper was shown in Fig. S9. The gel-like visual sensor prepared by agarose can make up for these deficiencies. Specifically, the HNT-CDs-Eu fluorescent nano-sensor was mixed with agarose and the obtained agarose gel-like nano-sensor showed strong blue fluorescence under the assistance of 245 nm UV light. In Fig. S10, the physical images of agarose gel from different perspectives and the fluorescence images before and after adding DPA were given. As shown in Fig. 5, with the increase of DPA concentration, the fluorescence color of the gel varied from blue to red. It was worth noting that in a small concentration range (0–5 μM), the agarose gelling sensor still presented a rapid visual qualitative and semi-quantitative recognition of low concentration DPA in addition to extremely sensitive color changes. Moreover, the diffusion of DPA solution on the surface of agarose gel can reach equilibrium within 10 s, with rapid and sensitive response and obvious fluorescence color change (Fig. S11). However, it was difficult to quantify DPA concentration only by visual analysis. Therefore, with the help of the color recognition application (App) of portable smartphones, the color values of red, green, and blue (RGB) in the fluorescent color image can be digitized and analyzed to achieve the semi-quantitative analysis of DPA. Since this fluorescent analysis strategy based on smart phone sensing platform has higher sensitivity and portability, it had been widely used in the detection of many targets [66–68]. Therefore, we also constructed a smart phone sensing platform based on polychromatic agarose gel. As shown in the right down of Fig. 5, in the concentration range of 0–20 μM, there was a significant relationship between the R/B value of the nano-sensor and the DPA concentration. The concentration range of DPA can be estimated according to the change of color ratio (R/B), which indicates that this method has excellent performance in DPA sensing.

The adsorption kinetics of polychromatic agarose gel was tested, which was shown in Fig. S12. 10 μM of DPA aqueous solution had a weak absorption peak at 270 nm, while after adding agarose gelled nanoprobe containing HNT-CDs-Eu, the absorption peak intensity of DPA was tested every 30 s in 2 min. The results showed that the absorption peak of DPA did not change significantly, which was due to the small adsorption amount of DPA by agarose gel. However, the addition of 5 μM DPA aqueous solution resulted the obvious color changes of agarose gelled nanoprobe. As shown in Fig. S11, red fluorescence was enhanced within 10 s after adding 5 μM DPA, and the change of fluorescence color was not obvious after 10 s, indicating that the diffusion of DPA aqueous solution on the surface of agarose gel reached equilibrium. In summary, the prepared agarose gel had a strong adsorption capacity for DPA aqueous solutions. Although the adsorption capacity was small, it was sufficient to cause obvious fluorescence color changes in a short time (< 10 s), indicating that it had high sensitivity to DPA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of fluorescence sensing system in logic decoder
ce:section.ce:para: In addition, based on the sensitive response of HNT-CDs-Eu probe to DPA and the tunable color change sensing process, the simplest innovatived analytical device “one-to-two decoder” was constructed to monitor the concentration level of DPA, which converted an input signal into two output signals. There were four output logic groups: NOT (0,1), YES (1,0), PASS 1 (1,1) and PASS 0 (0,0), which had the potential to respond to the increasing concentration of DPA. Fig. 6a and b showed the simple schematic diagram of one-to-two logic gate decoder and the definition truth table of input and output signals. For the input signal, the existence and nonexistence of DPA were defined as “1” and “0”, respectively. The normalized fluorescence intensity ratio signal of Eu3+ (I616) and CDs (I445) was used as dual output. According to the actual detection situation, when the fluorescence intensity of blue channel (I445) is closest to that of red channel (I616), the DPA dose (0.5 μM) was defined as the threshold. When there was no DPA in the detection system, the red light channel was closed, and the blue light channel was opened, resulting in output (0, 1). When DPA exists in the detection system, the red light channel was triggered, while the blue light channel remained constant, resulting in output (1, 1). The normalized fluorescence intensity changes of HNT-CDs-Eu sensing system in the range of 0–5 μM DPA concentration and the truth table was shown in Fig. 6c and d. Each concentration had a standard color, which can be observed in CIE (Fig. 4d) calculated using the emission spectra in Fig. 4a. According to the color and calculation, when the DPA concentration was less than 0.5 μM, the output signal combination is (0,1), which showed blue fluorescence. When the concentration of DPA was greater than 0.5 μM, the output signal combination was (1,1), and the change of fluorescence color from blue to red was more obvious with the increase of DPA concentration. The designed logic device can perfectly analyze the DPA concentrations through color discrimination.

In summary, an innovative ratiometric fluorescence nano-sensor (HNT-CDs-Eu) based on in-situ growth of CDs on natural clay HNT and grafting of rare earth europium ions was designed in this research, which showed superior selectivity, high sensitivity (detection limit as low as 5 nM) and rapid response time (30 s) in the detection of anthrax bacterial markers. The reliability of DPA detection in actual water environment was also reliable. In addition, the coloration process of agarose gel device indicated that it had high visualization as the fluorescence from blue to red could be adjusted. The synthesis strategy of this multi-color fluorescence sensing system had opened up a new idea for the design of nano-sensors based on clay matrix, and also provided the possibility for the application of on-site real-time detection of anthracnose and further diagnostic equipment for spore-derived diseases.

Xiangzhen Chen: Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Lei Jia: Methodology, Data curation, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Supervision. Lina Zhang: Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Yongxin Li: Investigation, Formal analysis. Jun Xu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partly supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. U1904168, 51773052, and 21805071), the Program for Science & Technology Innovation Talents in Universities of Henan Province (No. 20HASTIT015).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2022.128356.

ce:section.ce:para:

Chem. 90 (11) (2018) 7004–7011.
